candidates.description: Open storm drain/drainage inlet visible in the grass strip adjacent to the sidewalk in image 8 (Northwest). This is a square/rectangular constructed opening at the curb-grass junction with what appears to be a missing or damaged cover, showing visible depth. This is drainage infrastructure, not a pothole in the asphalt roadway.

The road surface shows multiple rectangular patch repairs and utility access covers (manholes) throughout the intersection. The patches appear as lighter or darker areas of asphalt where previous repairs were made. No actual potholes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, or jagged edges are visible.

The dark rectangular opening visible in images 1 and 9 at the curb edge is a storm drain/drainage inlet with a missing or damaged cover. It has straight, constructed edges typical of infrastructure rather than jagged asphalt failure. The opening sits precisely at the curb-sidewalk junction, a standard drainage placement location.

The large dark area visible in multiple images (particularly images 1, 7, 8, 9) is the characteristic Street View near-nadir stitching artifact - a flat, textureless gray-brown smear with no discernible pavement texture or edges. This is not actual road damage but a camera/processing artifact. The actual road surface visible in the clear portions of the images shows normal asphalt in fair condition.

The images show a commercial street in Panama City with parked vehicles, storefronts, and well-maintained asphalt. The prominent linear feature along the curb is a drainage grate/channel (yellow-painted metal grating) for stormwater management, not road damage. The road surface shows normal wear with some minor surface irregularities but no actual potholes with missing asphalt.

The location shows a commercial area parking lot and street with multiple parked vehicles including distinctive yellow taxis with checkered patterns. A linear drainage grate/gutter system is visible running along the curb edge in images 1, 3, and 9. The road surface appears to be in generally good condition with normal wear patterns but no potholes or significant asphalt damage visible.

The images show a commercial/retail area parking lot in Panama City with a prominent drainage grate/channel running along the curb line. The yellow-painted metal drainage grate is visible in multiple angles (images 1, 2, 3, 4, 9), which is constructed infrastructure, not pavement damage. The asphalt road surface appears to be in generally good condition with normal wear patterns.

The dark area visible in multiple images (particularly images 1, 8, and 9) is the characteristic near-nadir stitching artifact from Google Street View - a flat, textureless gray/brown smear with no discernible pavement details, edges, or depth. This is not real road damage but a processing artifact where the camera's imagery couldn't properly capture the area directly below/near the vehicle.

The downward-facing image (Image 9) shows a large gray/brown smear characteristic of Street View near-nadir stitching artifacts - this is not actual pavement damage but a rendering artifact. All other directional images show normal urban street scenes with intact asphalt pavement, parked vehicles, and commercial establishments.

The road surface shows normal urban wear with minor surface irregularities and some water pooling/wet areas visible across multiple angles. No discrete holes with missing asphalt, exposed base material, or jagged broken edges are visible. The pavement appears intact with typical wear patterns.

The location shows a construction site area with painted street markings reading '57 OBARRIO' and a 'Mini Design House' sign. The road surface is wet/muddy but shows no actual potholes. There is construction fencing, a parked scooter, and general construction activity but the asphalt itself does not have holes or missing material.

This location shows an urban street intersection in Panama City (Obarrio neighborhood based on signage). The road surface consists of asphalt with general wear patterns but no discrete potholes visible. There is normal surface deterioration, some uneven patches, and construction activity nearby but no holes with missing asphalt material.

Urban street in Panama City showing normal asphalt wear with no potholes visible. The road surface shows typical aging with some surface texture variation but no holes, depressions, or missing asphalt material. Multiple parked vehicles line the street, and paved sidewalks with brick pavers are visible.
